draft.introduction: Gregory the Great’s Pastoral Rule defines the qualities and duties required of a bishop, arguing that the care of souls is the “art of arts.” It became the standard manual for clergy throughout the medieval West.

translation.text: No one presumes to teach an art that he has not first mastered through study. How foolish therefore are those who assume the office of pastoral care without having first acquired the skill. The care of souls is the art of arts. Who then dares to teach this art without first having learned it?
translation.original_text: Nulla ars doceri praesumitur nisi intenta prius meditatione discatur

translator_notes.note: The phrase ars artium regimen animarum (the art of arts is the governance of souls) echoes Gregory of Nazianzus. Gregory the Great’s work was so valued that the Emperor Maurice had it translated into Greek, and Alfred the Great translated it into Anglo-Saxon.
